author: managePy
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Your Django app gets 10k requests/sec to a single API endpoint that queries the database. Response times spike to 5 seconds.

Walk me through how you debug and fix this without throwing more hardware at it. What's your first move?

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: First move: django-debug-toolbar or django-silk to find the actual bottleneck. 90% of the time it's N+1 queries. One .select_related() or .prefetch_related() call and suddenly you're doing 2 queries instead of 10,001. After that: add db indexes, then cache with Redis, THEN think about scaling.

comments.author: middlewareStack
comments.content: Don't sleep on Django's per-view cache decorator. @cache_page(60 * 15) on a read-heavy endpoint? Instant fix. 15 minutes of staleness is fine for most dashboards.

author: middlewareStack
content:
hot take: Django is "boring technology" and that's its superpower.

While you're debugging your Next.js edge function cold starts and wrestling with React Server Components hydration mismatches, my Django app has been running in production for 6 years without a single rewrite.

boring wins.

comments.author: templateTag
comments.content: This. Django templates are "boring" too. No build step. No hydration. No client/server mismatch bugs. Just HTML with variables. Sometimes boring IS the innovation. [days since JS broke something: 1]

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: The Django admin alone saves me 200+ hours per project. Try getting a full CRUD admin panel in Next.js without a third-party package and a weekend of your life.

author: templateTag
content:
If you're coming from React, Django's MVT will confuse you. Here's the cheat sheet:

Model = your Prisma schema
View = your API route handler (NOT the visual!)
Template = your JSX but server-rendered

The "V" in MVT is the controller logic, not the visual layer. Django named it "View" because it's what the user "views as a response." Confusing? Yes. But once it clicks, you're golden.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: And forms.py is like Zod + React Hook Form combined. Django gives you validation AND HTML rendering from one class. It's wild coming from the JS ecosystem where you need 3 packages for that.

author: queryset
content:
Django ORM tip that'll save your life:

.select_related() → ForeignKey (one query with JOIN)
.prefetch_related() → ManyToMany (two queries, joined in Python)

The N+1 problem is REAL and it's probably in your code right now. Run your tests with django-query-count and prepare to be horrified.
code:
# BAD: N+1 — one query per article for author
for article in Article.objects.all():  # 1 query
    print(article.author.name)          # N queries!

# GOOD: 1 query total with JOIN
for article in Article.objects.select_related('author'):
    print(article.author.name)  # already loaded!

# For ManyToMany:
for article in Article.objects.prefetch_related('tags'):
    print(article.tags.all())  # pre-fetched in batch

author: migrationRunner
content: This migration ran fine on my machine but crashed in production with "column does not exist." The table was already there. What went wrong?

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: The dependency points to 0001, not 0002! If 0002 added or renamed a column that 0003 depends on, production might run them in the wrong order. Always depend on the IMMEDIATELY PREVIOUS migration, not just initial. Django auto-generates this correctly — someone hand-edited this.

author: migrationRunner
content:
found this in #backend at 4:58pm on a Friday:

junior_dev: hey is it ok if I run migrate on prod real quick? just a small change
senior_dev: WHAT change
junior_dev: just adding a NOT NULL column with no default
senior_dev: DO NOT
junior_dev: too late it's running
senior_dev: ...
database_bot: Migration failed. Table locked. 47 pending transactions.
junior_dev: how do I undo
senior_dev: [is typing...] [stopped typing] [is typing...] [left the channel]

Always add a default value before making a column NOT NULL on a table with existing data. Or better: add the column as nullable first, backfill, then alter to NOT NULL.

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: Back in Django 1.4, we didn't even HAVE RunPython. You just ran raw SQL and prayed. Kids today don't know how good they have it with reversible migrations.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: The "47 pending transactions" is what gets me. That's 47 requests all waiting for a table lock that will never release because the migration is stuck. Every one of those becomes a timeout. The cascade is beautiful in a horrifying way.

author: managePy
content: TIL: Django's `get_or_create()` returns a TUPLE, not an object. It's (object, created). Every single beginner does `user = User.objects.get_or_create(email=x)` and then wonders why `user.name` throws an error. It's because user is (User, True). You want `user, created = ...` or `user, _ = ...`.

comments.author: templateTag
comments.content: JavaScript equivalent of this pain: fetch().then(res => res.json()) where people forget the second .json() call and try to read properties off a Response object. Tuple unpacking is just Python being Python.

author: managePy
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You're reviewing a PR from a JS developer who just joined your Django team. They wrote this Python. What feedback do you give?

Hint: three things are "technically valid" but not Pythonic. A senior Python dev would rewrite all three lines differently.
code:
# Their code:
if len(users) > 0:
    filtered = list(filter(lambda u: u.active == True, users))
    msg = "Found " + str(len(filtered)) + " active users"

# Pythonic version:
if users:
    filtered = [u for u in users if u.active]
    msg = f"Found {len(filtered)} active users"

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: The list comprehension isn't just style—it's faster too. filter() + lambda creates two function call overheads per element. The comprehension compiles to a tighter loop. Measured it once: 35% faster on 10k items.

comments.author: templateTag
comments.content: f-strings are the one thing in Python that make me feel like the language designers actually use their own language. Unlike certain JS template literal decisions I could name. [days since JS broke something: 2]

author: templateTag
content:
hot take: Python's indentation-as-syntax is actually BETTER than curly braces.

In JS, your code can lie. It can look properly indented while the braces tell a different story. In Python, what you see IS what the interpreter sees.

JS devs complain about significant whitespace for exactly one week. Then they realize they were already indenting their code correctly—Python just made it mandatory.

no build step. no transpiler. no prettier config debates. just... indent correctly.

[days since JS broke something: 0. Prettier 4.0 dropped and half the ecosystem's formatting changed overnight.]

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: Back in Django 1.4, we had a production outage because someone mixed tabs and spaces. Python 3 made that a hard error. The language learned from its mistakes—unlike some ecosystems that just add another config file.

comments.author: middlewareStack
comments.content:
whitespace defines form
like silence shapes a melody
structure IS the code

author: managePy
content:
PRO TIP: Python f-strings vs JS template literals—same idea, different superpower.

Python f-strings can do inline expressions, formatting, and even debugging with the = sign. Most JS devs don't know about that last one.
code:
# Python f-strings
name = "Django"
version = 4.2
print(f"Using {name} v{version}")        # Using Django v4.2
print(f"Pi is {3.14159:.2f}")             # Pi is 3.14
print(f"{name.upper()!r}")                # 'DJANGO'
print(f"{version = }")                    # version = 4.2  (debug!)
print(f"{'yes' if version > 4 else 'no'}") # yes

// JavaScript template literals
const name = "Express";
const version = 4.18;
console.log(`Using ${name} v${version}`);  // same basic idea
console.log(`Pi is ${(3.14159).toFixed(2)}`); // method call needed
// No debug shorthand. No format specs. No !r repr.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: The f"{variable = }" trick is incredible for debugging ORM queries. f"{qs.query = }" prints the actual SQL. Saved me hours. (Query count for this tip: 0. A personal best.)

author: queryset
content:
SPOT THE BUG: A JS developer wrote their first Python function. It "works" but has a classic Python gotcha that will cause nightmares in production.

Hint: call the function twice without arguments.
code:
def add_item(item, items=[]):
    items.append(item)
    return items

result1 = add_item("django")
print(result1)  # ['django']

result2 = add_item("flask")
print(result2)  # Expected: ['flask']
                # Actual:   ['django', 'flask']  !!!

# Fix: use None as default, create list inside
def add_item(item, items=None):
    if items is None:
        items = []
    items.append(item)
    return items

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: This one bites EVERYONE exactly once. The default list is created when the function is DEFINED, not when it's called. It's the same list object every time. In JS, default params are evaluated per call. In Python, they're evaluated once. Respect the difference.

comments.author: migrationRunner
comments.content: Migration Horror Stories, Appendix B: someone used a mutable default in a data migration's RunPython function. It accumulated state across 40,000 rows. The migration took 6 hours and produced wrong data for every row after the first. I still have nightmares.

author: middlewareStack
content:
MENTAL MODEL: Python's truthiness vs JavaScript's truthiness

In JS, truthiness is a minefield of WAT moments. In Python, the rules are almost... elegant.

Falsy in Python: False, None, 0, 0.0, "", [], {}, set(), ()
Everything else: truthy.

That's it. No "0" being truthy while 0 is falsy. No [] being truthy (it's falsy in Python!). No NaN because Python uses None as its "absence of value."

The philosophy: empty collections are falsy. Populated ones are truthy. Absence is falsy. Presence is truthy.

request in, boolean out. The universe is middleware.

comments.author: templateTag
comments.content: In JS, [] == false is true but [] is truthy in an if statement. I have that burned into my memory alongside my therapist's phone number. Python just... makes sense.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: This is why `if queryset:` works in Django. An empty QuerySet is falsy. A populated one is truthy. But careful—`if queryset` evaluates the ENTIRE query. Use `if queryset.exists()` for efficiency. (1 query vs loading all rows.)

author: queryset
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Explain the difference between a list comprehension and using map/filter in Python. When would you choose one over the other?

This is a culture question. The answer reveals whether you think in Python or just write Python-shaped code.
code:
# JS developer instinct:
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
evens_doubled = list(map(lambda x: x * 2, filter(lambda x: x % 2 == 0, nums)))
# [4, 8]

# Pythonic:
evens_doubled = [x * 2 for x in nums if x % 2 == 0]
# [4, 8]

# When map() wins: applying an existing named function
names = ["django", "flask", "fastapi"]
upper_names = list(map(str.upper, names))
# cleaner than [n.upper() for n in names]? debatable.

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: The right answer: comprehensions for 90% of cases. map() when you already have a named function and the comprehension would just be `[f(x) for x in items]`. If you're writing a lambda inside map(), stop. Use a comprehension. There's a battery for that.

comments.author: middlewareStack
comments.content: A comprehension transforms input to output in one breath. map and filter are two steps pretending to be one. Simplicity is its own middleware.

author: queryset
content:
PRO TIP: Python dict comprehensions are the secret weapon JS devs don't know they're missing.

In JS you'd use Object.fromEntries() with map(). In Python, it's one clean expression.

comments.author: templateTag
comments.content: Object.fromEntries(users.map(u => [u.id, u.name])) is the JS equivalent. I had to google it. Again. Python's version I can actually remember. No build step, no problems, no googling.

author: managePy
content:
INTERVIEW Q: What's the difference between *args and **kwargs in Python? Give a real-world example where you'd use both.

Back in Django 1.4, we passed everything as positional arguments and just hoped for the best. kwargs was a revelation. Like going from "pass me that thing" to "pass me the salt."

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: Real-world Django use: almost every class-based view method signature is `def get(self, request, *args, **kwargs)`. If you don't understand *args/**kwargs, you can't read Django source code. And if you can't read Django source code, how many unnecessary queries are you writing? (Rhetorical. It's a lot.)

author: managePy
content:
hot take: Python's `None` is better designed than JavaScript's null/undefined split.

JS has TWO ways to say "nothing": null (intentional absence) and undefined (accidental absence). This creates an entire category of bugs that don't exist in Python.

Python has `None`. One value. One way to check it (`is None`). No "is it null or undefined or void 0 or NaN" guessing game.

Every time I see `value == null` in JS (which catches both null AND undefined), I think: you just proved my point. You need a special operator to paper over a design mistake.

comments.author: templateTag
comments.content:
JS optional chaining: obj?.prop ?? 'default'
Python: getattr(obj, 'prop', 'default')

One language needed new syntax. The other had a built-in function since the beginning. [days since JS broke something: 1]

comments.author: middlewareStack
comments.content: None is the silence between notes. null and undefined are two different silences that somehow make different sounds. One path through the middleware. One way to express nothing.

author: templateTag
content:
PRO TIP: Python's ternary is backwards from JS and that trips up EVERYONE.

Once you read it as English—"give me X if condition, else Y"—it actually reads more naturally. But the first week is rough.

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: Guido van Rossum resisted adding a ternary for YEARS. When Python finally got it, he made it read like English on purpose. That's the Python philosophy—code is read more often than it's written. There's a battery for readability.

author: managePy
content: SPOT THE BUG: A JS dev wrote this Python and says "it works but sometimes gives wrong answers." What's the issue?

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: Setting aside the logic bug—you're checking permissions with string comparison? Where's the role coming from? If it's user input, someone is sending role="admin" in their request body right now. Parameterize or perish.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: The `in` operator is the right call here. It's also faster—one lookup instead of chained comparisons. Not that it matters at this scale, but good habits start with small decisions. (Query count: 0, but security count: also 0. Listen to Bobby.)

author: queryset
content:
TIL: Python type hints have ZERO runtime performance cost. They're completely ignored by the interpreter. They exist for mypy, IDE autocomplete, and your future self reading the code 6 months later.

Coming from TypeScript where types are compiled away, this feels familiar. But Python went further—type hints are optional forever. No tsconfig. No strict mode. Add them where they help, skip them where they don't.

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: Django 4.1+ has full type stub support. django-stubs makes your IDE understand QuerySet types, model fields, everything. Type hints went from "nice to have" to "how did we live without this." There's a battery for that now.

comments.author: migrationRunner
comments.content: Type hints caught a bug in our data migration last month. A RunPython function was receiving a string ID where it expected an int. mypy flagged it. Without types, that would have been a Migration Horror Story, season 3.

author: managePy
content:
MENTAL MODEL: Python dicts are NOT JavaScript objects. They're better.

In JS, object keys are always strings (or Symbols). In Python, dict keys can be any immutable type: strings, numbers, tuples, even frozensets.

JS objects mix data storage with behavior (methods, prototypes, __proto__). Python dicts are pure data. No prototype chain. No hidden properties. No `hasOwnProperty()` dance.

Think of a Python dict as a JSON object that grew up and got a computer science degree.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: A Python dict is basically a hash table JOIN between keys and values. The hash function is the join condition. I'll show myself out.

comments.author: templateTag
comments.content: In Django templates, you access dict values with dot notation: {{ user.name }}. It looks like JS object access but it's actually calling __getitem__ under the hood. No build step needed to understand that.

author: migrationRunner
content:
TIL: Python has a walrus operator `:=` that assigns AND returns a value in one expression. It's like doing `const x = value` inside an if condition in JS.

Found this while debugging a migration that needed to check AND use a queryset result without evaluating it twice.
code:
# Without walrus: evaluate twice
data = get_data()
if data:
    process(data)

# With walrus: evaluate once
if data := get_data():
    process(data)

# Great for while loops
while chunk := file.read(8192):
    process(chunk)

# And comprehension filtering
results = [clean for item in items if (clean := transform(item)) is not None]

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: I use this with QuerySets constantly. `if qs := Model.objects.filter(...).first():` saves a query vs checking .exists() then .first(). One query instead of two. Lifetime N+1 fixes: 2,848.

author: queryset
content:
hot take: if you're coming from JavaScript and you reach for a for loop in Python, you're probably doing it wrong.

Python has a comprehension, a generator expression, a built-in, or a standard library function for almost everything you'd write a loop for.

The loop isn't wrong. It's just... not how we do things here.

how many queries though? zero. this is a philosophy post.
code:
# JS instinct: loop and accumulate
# total = 0
# for item in items:
#     total += item.price

# Python: just use sum()
total = sum(item.price for item in items)

# JS instinct: loop and check
# found = False
# for item in items:
#     if item.active:
#         found = True
#         break

# Python: just use any()
found = any(item.active for item in items)

# Also: all(), min(), max(), sorted(), enumerate(), zip()

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: There's a battery for that. There's literally always a battery for that. sum(), any(), all(), min(), max()—these aren't clever tricks, they're the standard library doing its job. Read the docs like scripture and ye shall be rewarded.

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: This is the same philosophy as D3's data joins—declare WHAT you want, not HOW to loop through it. data in, meaning out. Python comprehensions and D3 selections are spiritual cousins. (Draft blog post now at 14,200 words.)

author: migrationRunner
content:
PRO TIP: Python tuple unpacking is destructuring on steroids. If you're from JS, this will feel familiar—then surprise you.

Every time you see a tuple return in Django (like get_or_create), you need this.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: The `first, *middle, last` pattern is underrated. Parsing CSV headers? `header, *data_rows, footer = rows`. Clean, readable, zero extra queries... wait, this isn't even a database thing. I need a hobby.

author: middlewareStack
content:
POLL: What does this Python expression evaluate to?

`result = [x**2 for x in range(5) if x % 2]`

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: It's [1, 9]. range(5) gives 0,1,2,3,4. The `if x % 2` filters to odd numbers (1, 3) because 0 is falsy. Then squares them: 1, 9. The filter runs BEFORE the transform. Read it left to right: generate, filter, transform.

comments.author: middlewareStack
comments.content: A comprehension is middleware for data. The iterable is the request. The condition is the filter layer. The expression is the response transformation. request in, list out.

author: managePy
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Explain Python decorators to someone who knows JavaScript higher-order functions.

Back in Django 1.4, we used decorators for everything. @login_required, @csrf_exempt, @cache_page. They haven't changed because they didn't need to. Good design ages well.

comments.author: middlewareStack
comments.content:
functions wrapped in care
decorators fold like dough
middleware of code

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: Django's @cached_property is the decorator that saves the most queries in my experience. It computes an attribute once and caches it on the instance. Instead of hitting the DB every time you access a property, it's one query per object lifetime. Beautiful.

author: middlewareStack
content:
hot take: Python generators are the most underappreciated feature for web developers.

You have 10 million rows. You don't need them all in memory. You need them one at a time. A generator gives you a pipeline—each item flows through only when asked for.

It's middleware for data. Each yield is a response. Each next() is a request.

request in, response out, memory stays flat.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: Django's QuerySet.iterator() IS a generator under the hood. Instead of loading 10k objects into memory, it streams them from the database. I've seen this single method drop memory usage from 2GB to 50MB. Generators aren't academic—they're production survival.

author: templateTag
content: SPOT THE BUG: A JS developer is trying to build a string in Python. This runs without errors but the senior dev is NOT happy. What's wrong and why?
code:
# Building a URL with query params
base = "https://api.example.com/users"
params = {"page": 2, "sort": "name", "active": True}

url = base + "?"
for key in params:
    url = url + key + "=" + str(params[key]) + "&"
url = url[:-1]  # remove trailing &

# Result: https://api.example.com/users?page=2&sort=name&active=True

# Problems:
# 1. String concatenation in a loop creates a new string each time (O(n^2))
# 2. No URL encoding! What if a value has spaces or special chars?
# 3. Python has urllib.parse.urlencode() for this exact thing

# Fix:
from urllib.parse import urlencode
url = f"{base}?{urlencode(params)}"
# https://api.example.com/users?page=2&sort=name&active=True

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: There's a battery for that. There is ALWAYS a battery for that. urllib.parse, json, csv, pathlib, datetime—before you write a utility function, check the standard library. Python's stdlib is its greatest superpower.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: The missing URL encoding is a security issue too. If any of those params come from user input, you're one step away from an injection vector. urlencode() handles escaping. Your hand-rolled loop doesn't. Parameterize or perish.

author: queryset
content:
INTERVIEW Q: What's the difference between a Python set and a list? When would you use each?

I ask this in every interview. The candidates who mention O(1) lookup get a follow-up. The ones who mention frozenset get an offer.
code:
# List: ordered, duplicates OK, O(n) lookup
users = ["manny", "tanya", "miggy", "manny"]
"manny" in users  # True, but checks EVERY element

# Set: unordered, no duplicates, O(1) lookup
active_ids = {101, 202, 303, 404}
202 in active_ids  # True, instant hash lookup

# Real-world: checking membership
# Bad: O(n) per check, O(n*m) total
banned = ["spam@bot.com", "hack@evil.com"]
for user in all_users:
    if user.email in banned:  # scans entire list each time
        block(user)

# Good: O(1) per check, O(n) total
banned = {"spam@bot.com", "hack@evil.com"}
for user in all_users:
    if user.email in banned:  # hash lookup
        block(user)

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: The frozenset follow-up: it's an immutable set, so it's hashable, so you can use it as a dict key or put it inside another set. When someone knows that, they understand Python's "hashable = immutable" contract. That's deep language knowledge.

author: middlewareStack

comments.author: templateTag
comments.content: Django's view decorators are exactly this. @login_required wraps your view in an auth check. @csrf_exempt removes the CSRF layer. @require_http_methods(["GET"]) filters by method. Stack them and your view is protected before it even runs. No JavaScript framework needed.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: And @cache_page(60*15) is the decorator equivalent of "I already answered this question 14 minutes ago, go read that." Saves queries. Saves compute. Saves my sanity. (Queries saved: incalculable.)

author: queryset
content:
PRO TIP: Python's collections module has data structures that solve problems JS devs reach for lodash to solve.

Stop writing helper functions. The standard library already did it.

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: There's a battery for that. defaultdict alone has saved me thousands of lines of if/else boilerplate over the years. Counter.most_common() is what every analytics dashboard needs but nobody knows exists.

author: managePy
content:
INTERVIEW Q: What's the difference between `__str__` and `__repr__` in Python? Which one does Django use in the admin panel?

Back in Django 1.4, we had to remember to define __unicode__ instead of __str__. Kids today don't know how good they have it with Python 3.

The answer I want: __str__ is for users, __repr__ is for developers. Django admin uses __str__.
code:
class Article:
    def __init__(self, title, author):
        self.title = title
        self.author = author

    def __str__(self):
        # Human-readable: what users see
        return self.title

    def __repr__(self):
        # Developer-readable: what the debugger shows
        return f"Article(title={self.title!r}, author={self.author!r})"

a = Article("Why Python Wins", "Manny")
print(a)      # Why Python Wins          (__str__)
print([a])    # [Article(title='Why...')]  (__repr__)

# Django admin dropdown: shows __str__
# Django shell:          shows __repr__
# f-string {obj}:        calls __str__
# f-string {obj!r}:      calls __repr__

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: Protip: always define __repr__ on your models during development. When you're debugging a QuerySet in the shell and all you see is `<Article: Article object (1)>` repeated 50 times, you'll wish you had. (Time wasted reading unhelpful reprs this year: 47 hours. Approximately.)

author: queryset
content:
hot take: Python's `@dataclass` makes 80% of custom `__init__` methods pointless.

If your class is mostly "store these values and let me access them," you don't need to hand-write __init__, __repr__, __eq__, or __hash__. Dataclass generates all of them.

It's like TypeScript interfaces but they actually DO something at runtime.

how many queries though? zero. but how many lines of boilerplate saved? dozens.

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: Note: Django models are NOT dataclasses—they have their own metaclass magic. But for service layer objects, DTOs, and config objects? Dataclasses are the battery. There's a battery for that.

comments.author: migrationRunner
comments.content: See that `field(default_factory=list)`? That's how dataclasses solve the mutable default argument bug from django-013. It creates a new list for each instance. The language learned from its own gotcha.

author: middlewareStack
content:
MENTAL MODEL: Python classes for JS developers

Forget everything complicated about JS classes (prototype chains, `this` binding, arrow functions in constructors). Python classes are simpler.

`self` is always explicit. It's the first parameter of every method. No guessing what `this` refers to. No `.bind()` needed. No arrow function tricks.

`__init__` is the constructor. But it's not called "constructor" because Python separates object CREATION (__new__) from object INITIALIZATION (__init__). 99% of the time, you only care about __init__.

The method receives the instance.
The instance carries the state.
State in, behavior out.
request in, response out, nothing else matters.

comments.author: templateTag
comments.content: "No guessing what `this` refers to" is the most beautiful sentence in programming. In JS, `this` is a riddle wrapped in a mystery wrapped in a setTimeout. In Python, `self` is just... the object. That's it. [days since JS broke something: 0. Someone used `this` in a callback again.]

author: queryset
content: SPOT THE BUG: This class looks fine to a JS developer but has a classic Python gotcha. Every instance shares the same list. Why?

link.label: class vs instance variables in Lesson 4 →

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: In JS, class fields with `tasks = []` create a new array per instance. In Python, `tasks = []` at class level is shared. It's a fundamental difference. The rule: if it should be unique per instance, put it in __init__. If it should be shared (like a counter), put it at class level.

comments.author: middlewareStack
comments.content:
class level, all share
instance level, each their own
scope defines the self

author: templateTag
content:
Python is slow. Full stop. If you care about performance, use Go or Rust.

I ran a fibonacci benchmark and Python was 100x slower than Node.js. Why would anyone choose a slow language for web development in 2026?

[days since JS broke something: this post is about to break MY ratio]

comments.author: managePy
comments.content:
Tanya I love you but this take is incomplete.

1. Your web app is I/O bound, not CPU bound. It's waiting for the database, not computing fibonacci.
2. Instagram serves 2 BILLION users on Django/Python.
3. The bottleneck is almost always queries, not language speed.
4. For CPU-heavy work: numpy (C), pandas (C), or Celery offloading.

"Python is slow" is technically true for raw computation and completely irrelevant for 95% of web development. The database is the bottleneck. Always.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: I profiled a "slow" Django app last week. Python execution time: 3ms. Database queries: 4,200ms across 847 queries. The language wasn't slow. The N+1 problem was. Fix the queries, not the language. (Lifetime N+1 fixes: 2,849.)

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: This is like blaming your car for traffic. The car isn't slow—it's the road (network), the traffic lights (database), and the other cars (concurrent requests). Optimize the route, not the engine. There's a join for that—specifically, the join between your app and its actual bottleneck.

author: queryset
content:
INTERVIEW Q: What's the difference between inheritance and composition in Python? When would you choose one over the other in a Django project?

The answer that gets you the job: "Inheritance for 'is-a' relationships (User is-a Model). Composition for 'has-a' relationships (User has-a EmailService). Django uses both, but composition is usually the better default."

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: Django's class-based views use inheritance heavily (CreateView is-a FormView is-a View). It works, but the MRO (method resolution order) can get confusing with multiple mixins. When someone asks "which method gets called?" and the answer requires drawing a graph, you've hit the inheritance ceiling. That's when composition shines.

comments.author: middlewareStack
comments.content:
Inheritance chains are like nested middleware—each layer modifies behavior. Composition is like choosing which middleware to load from a config. Both transform the request. The difference is whether the layers are baked in or pluggable.

one path is fixed
the other shifts like water flows
both carry the load

author: queryset
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You inherit a Django API endpoint that returns a list of 100 orders, each with its customer info and line items. The page takes 8 seconds to load. django-debug-toolbar shows 1,204 queries.

How do you get it under 10 queries without changing the serializer output?

This is the single most common Django performance question. If you can't answer it, you're not ready.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: Answer: `Order.objects.select_related('customer').prefetch_related(Prefetch('items', queryset=OrderItem.objects.select_related('product')))[:100]`. Three queries. Down from 1,204. Lifetime N+1 count: 2,848.

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: Back in Django 1.4, we didn't have Prefetch objects. You just prefetch_related'd and hoped. The nested select_related inside a Prefetch is a game changer that most people don't know about.

author: managePy
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You need to add a new feature to a Django project but you're not sure where to put it. The project has 12 apps already.

Do you:
A) Add it to the closest existing app
B) Create a new app with `startapp`
C) Dump it in a `utils.py` somewhere

The real answer is: it depends on whether this feature has its own models. If it has models, it's an app. If it's just helper functions, it can live in a shared app. If you're unsure, start with a new app — it's cheap to create and expensive to untangle later.

This is like asking "do I make a new Next.js route group or add to an existing one" — but Django apps are more self-contained. Each app is its own mini-package with models, views, urls, tests.

comments.author: migrationRunner
comments.content: And if you create a new app, add it to INSTALLED_APPS BEFORE you create any models. I've seen people forget, run makemigrations, get nothing, panic, and hand-write migrations. Did you add it to INSTALLED_APPS? Did you run makemigrations?

comments.author: middlewareStack
comments.content:
an app is born
models views and urls breathe
INSTALLED_APPS waits

author: queryset
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Your Django view returns a count of articles per category. It works but it's slow on 500k rows.

Candidate A writes a Python loop that counts manually.
Candidate B writes an ORM query with .annotate().

Candidate B gets the job. Here's why:
code:
# Candidate A: O(n) in Python, loads 500k rows into memory
counts = {}
for article in Article.objects.all():
    cat = article.category.name
    counts[cat] = counts.get(cat, 0) + 1

# Candidate B: O(1) in Python, database does the work
from django.db.models import Count
counts = Category.objects.annotate(
    article_count=Count('article')
).values('name', 'article_count')

# The database is BUILT for this. Let it do its job.
# Candidate A: ~500k queries (N+1 on category)
# Candidate B: 1 query with GROUP BY

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: This is just GROUP BY + COUNT(*) under the hood. The ORM writes the SQL so you don't have to. But knowing what SQL it generates makes you dangerous in the best way.

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: There's a battery for that. Django's aggregation API is one of the most underused features. Count, Sum, Avg, Max, Min — all built in. Stop writing Python loops to do database work.

author: middlewareStack
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Explain what happens between a user pressing Enter on a URL and your Django view function executing. Every layer.

Most candidates jump straight to "the view runs." The ones who get hired describe the middleware stack.

The request enters. SecurityMiddleware checks HTTPS. SessionMiddleware loads the session. AuthenticationMiddleware attaches the user. CsrfViewMiddleware validates the token. Your URL resolver finds the view. The view runs.

Then it ALL reverses on the way out. Response goes back UP through every middleware. Each layer can modify, log, or reject.

It's an onion. It's a stack of pancakes. It's the most elegant pattern in the framework and nobody talks about it in interviews.

comments.author: middlewareStack
comments.content:
request descends through
each layer a quiet gate
response climbs back home

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: And if you're wondering how many queries happen before your view even runs: SessionMiddleware hits the DB for the session, AuthenticationMiddleware hits it for the user. That's 2 queries minimum before your code even starts. Keep that in mind when counting.

author: managePy
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You're building a blog. A Post can have many Tags and a Tag can belong to many Posts. How do you model this in Django?

Bonus: How is this different from the same relationship in Prisma?

In Prisma you need an explicit join table or implicit many-to-many. In Django, `ManyToManyField` handles the join table for you automatically. You never see it. You never migrate it. It just works.

That's batteries included.
code:
# Django: implicit join table
class Tag(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=50)

class Post(models.Model):
    title = models.CharField(max_length=200)
    tags = models.ManyToManyField(Tag, related_name='posts')

# Usage:
post.tags.add(tag1, tag2)
post.tags.all()          # all tags for this post
tag1.posts.all()         # all posts with this tag (reverse!)

# Prisma equivalent would need:
# model PostTag { post Post @relation(...) tag Tag @relation(...) }
# Django just... does it.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: And when you need the join table (extra fields like `added_date`), use `through=`. But don't reach for it until you need it. YAGNI applies to join tables too.

comments.author: migrationRunner
comments.content: Fun fact: Django creates a migration for the join table automatically. It's named like `post_tags` and it's managed entirely by the framework. Just don't forget to run makemigrations after adding the field.

author: queryset
content:
INTERVIEW Q: What's the difference between `filter(condition_a, condition_b)` and `filter(condition_a).filter(condition_b)` in Django?

Most people say "they're the same." Most people are wrong — sometimes.

For simple field lookups on the same model, yes, identical. But for multi-valued relationships (like filtering on related ManyToMany), chaining `.filter()` creates separate JOINs while comma-separated conditions use ONE JOIN.

This is a subtle distinction that has caused real production bugs at Stacksworth Inc. An endpoint returned duplicate results because someone chained filters on a M2M relationship and got multiple JOINs producing repeated rows.
code:
# These are the SAME:
Article.objects.filter(status='published', author__active=True)
Article.objects.filter(status='published').filter(author__active=True)

# These are DIFFERENT with M2M:
# "Posts with a tag named 'python' AND that same tag created in 2024"
Post.objects.filter(tags__name='python', tags__created__year=2024)

# "Posts with ANY tag named 'python' AND ANY tag created in 2024"
# (could be different tags!)
Post.objects.filter(tags__name='python').filter(tags__created__year=2024)

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: Under the hood: comma = one JOIN with AND in the ON clause. Chaining = two separate JOINs. The SQL tells the story if you call .query on the queryset. There's a join for that explanation.

author: managePy
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You join a team. The Django project has ONE app called `main` with 47 models in models.py, 2,000 lines of views, and a urls.py that makes you question your career choices.

How do you refactor without breaking production?

Step 1: Don't panic. This is every 3-year-old Django project.
Step 2: New features get new apps. Stop adding to `main`.
Step 3: Extract one model at a time with careful migration surgery.
Step 4: Use Django's `app_label` Meta option during transition.

At Stacksworth Inc., our monolith has been running for 6 years. We refactored from 1 app to 8 over a year. Zero downtime. The Django admin alone made it worth the effort — each app gets its own admin section.

comments.author: migrationRunner
comments.content: Step 3 is where nightmares live. Moving a model between apps means creating a new migration in the target app, a SeparateDatabaseAndState operation in the source app, and praying to whatever deity handles foreign key constraints. I've done this 11 times. Cried during 4 of them.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: And while you're refactoring, add select_related to every view that touches the models being moved. I guarantee there are N+1 queries hiding in that 2,000-line views.py. Guarantee it.

author: templateTag
content:
hot take: Django templates get called "limited" by people who've never actually hit those limits.

"But you can't write Python in templates!" Correct. That's a FEATURE. Your templates should be dumb. Your views should be smart. Separation of concerns isn't a suggestion.

React taught an entire generation that mixing logic and markup is fine. Django said "no" 20 years ago and it's still right.

[days since JS broke something: 0. Vite 7 just dropped with breaking config changes.]

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: Back in Django 1.4, the template language was even MORE limited. And you know what? Production apps ran fine. The limitations force you to put logic where it belongs: in the view or a custom template tag. There's a battery for that.

comments.author: middlewareStack
comments.content: Logic in templates is like performing surgery in the waiting room. The view is the operating theater. The template is the discharge summary.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: Also: template tags can't accidentally trigger N+1 queries if the data is already resolved in the view. Another underrated benefit of "dumb templates."

author: managePy
content:
hot take: the Django admin is the most underrated tool in web development.

You define your models. You register them. You get:
- Full CRUD interface
- Search, filter, sort
- Inline editing for related objects
- Permissions per model per user
- Audit history
- Custom actions

For free. No code beyond `admin.site.register(MyModel)`.

In Next.js you'd need: a UI library, form handling, auth, RBAC, audit logging, and a weekend. The admin alone is worth the framework.
code:
# This is ALL you need:
# admin.py
from django.contrib import admin
from .models import Article, Category

@admin.register(Article)
class ArticleAdmin(admin.ModelAdmin):
    list_display = ['title', 'author', 'status', 'created']
    list_filter = ['status', 'category']
    search_fields = ['title', 'body']
    prepopulated_fields = {'slug': ('title',)}

# That's it. Full admin panel. Done.

comments.author: templateTag
comments.content: No build step required for the admin either. It's server-rendered HTML that just works. No npm install. No webpack config. No hydration. It loads in milliseconds on a 2015 laptop.

comments.author: migrationRunner
comments.content: The admin also shows you your migration status. It's how I caught a junior dev who had 3 unapplied migrations in production. The admin page showed warnings. Did they run makemigrations? They did not.

author: queryset
content:
hot take: `.all()` is the most dangerous method in the Django ORM.

Not because it's broken. Because it's lazy. It does nothing until you iterate. And by the time you iterate inside a template or a loop, you've already lost.

`Article.objects.all()` doesn't hit the database.
`list(Article.objects.all())` does.
`{% for a in articles %}` does.

Lazy evaluation is a feature. But it's a feature that lets you write code that LOOKS fine and then generates 10,000 queries at render time.

how many queries though?

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: This is why django-debug-toolbar should be in every project's dev dependencies from day one. Not "when we have performance issues." From. Day. One.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: And when you do .all() on a table with nullable foreign keys, each None becomes an invisible landmine. `article.author.name` doesn't just N+1 — it also raises AttributeError on None. The absence strikes twice.

author: middlewareStack
content:
hot take: settings.py is the most honest configuration file in web development.

Next.js has next.config.js, .env.local, .env.production, .env.development, vercel.json, and sometimes middleware.ts for config. That's 6 files minimum.

Django has settings.py. One file. It's Python, so you can do logic. It's explicit. Every setting is documented. You can read it top to bottom and understand your entire app's behavior.

Transparency is a feature. The request deserves to know what world it's entering.

comments.author: templateTag
comments.content: And when something breaks in production, you check ONE file. Not a chain of .env inheritance where .env.production.local overrides .env.production which overrides .env.local which overrides .env. [days since JS config broke something: 0]

author: templateTag
content:
hot take: if your Django project needs a JavaScript build step, you've already lost.

HTMX + Django templates = 95% of what an SPA gives you with 5% of the complexity.

- Partial page updates? hx-swap.
- Form submission without reload? hx-post.
- Real-time search? hx-trigger with a debounce.
- Loading indicators? hx-indicator.

No node_modules. No bundle size. No hydration mismatch. No "it works in dev but not in production because the build optimizer tree-shook the thing you needed."

no build step, no problems.

[days since JS broke something: 2. A new record!]

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: At Stacksworth Inc., we replaced a React SPA with Django templates + HTMX in 2024. The frontend codebase went from 42,000 lines to 6,000. Time-to-interactive dropped by 3 seconds. The React devs were upset for a week, then relieved.

comments.author: middlewareStack
comments.content: HTMX respects the request/response cycle. The server renders HTML. The client swaps it in. No intermediate JSON serialization layer pretending to be something it's not. Request in, HTML out. As it should be.

author: managePy
content:
Django tip: `python manage.py shell` is your REPL for testing ORM queries, but `shell_plus` from django-extensions is 10x better.

It auto-imports ALL your models. No more typing `from myapp.models import User, Article, Tag` every single time.

Think of it like the browser console but for your Django backend. You can test queries, create objects, debug relationships — all interactively.
code:
# Install:
# pip install django-extensions
# Add 'django_extensions' to INSTALLED_APPS

# Regular shell:
$ python manage.py shell
>>> from articles.models import Article  # manual import
>>> from users.models import User        # manual import
>>> Article.objects.count()

# shell_plus:
$ python manage.py shell_plus
# Auto-imports ALL models from ALL apps
>>> Article.objects.count()  # just works
>>> User.objects.filter(is_active=True).count()  # just works

# Pro move: shell_plus --print-sql
# Shows the actual SQL for every ORM query you run

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: shell_plus --print-sql is how I found 6 N+1 queries in a single view last week. Run your view's queryset in the shell, watch the SQL scroll by, count the queries. If you see the same query repeated with different IDs, that's your N+1. Lifetime count: 2,853.

author: queryset
content:
Django ORM tip: F() objects let you reference model fields in queries without loading them into Python.

This matters because the operation happens in the DATABASE, not in your app server. Atomic. Fast. No race conditions.
code:
from django.db.models import F

# BAD: race condition if two requests hit simultaneously
article = Article.objects.get(id=1)
article.view_count = article.view_count + 1  # read, then write
article.save()

# GOOD: atomic database operation
Article.objects.filter(id=1).update(view_count=F('view_count') + 1)

# F() works in filters too:
# Find articles where comments > likes (controversial!)
Article.objects.filter(comment_count__gt=F('like_count'))

# And in annotations:
from django.db.models import ExpressionWrapper, FloatField
Article.objects.annotate(
    engagement_ratio=ExpressionWrapper(
        F('comment_count') * 1.0 / F('view_count'),
        output_field=FloatField()
    )
)

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: F() expressions are also immune to SQL injection because they never interpolate user input. The database resolves the field reference internally. One of the few times the ORM makes your code both faster AND more secure.

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: The race condition in the BAD example is real. At Stacksworth Inc., we had a view counter that lost ~15% of counts during traffic spikes because of exactly this. One F() expression fixed it. There's a battery for that.

author: migrationRunner
content:
Migration tip that will save you in production: ALWAYS test migrations on a copy of production data before deploying.

Your local database has 50 rows. Production has 5 million. That `AddField` with a default runs instantly locally. In production? Table lock for 8 minutes.

Here's the safe pattern for adding a NOT NULL field to a large table:
code:
# Step 1: Add as nullable (fast, no table rewrite)
migrations.AddField(
    model_name='order',
    name='tracking_number',
    field=models.CharField(max_length=100, null=True),
)

# Step 2: Data migration to backfill (separate migration!)
def backfill_tracking(apps, schema_editor):
    Order = apps.get_model('orders', 'Order')
    # Batch update to avoid memory issues
    while Order.objects.filter(tracking_number__isnull=True).exists():
        batch = Order.objects.filter(
            tracking_number__isnull=True
        )[:1000].values_list('id', flat=True)
        Order.objects.filter(id__in=list(batch)).update(
            tracking_number='LEGACY'
        )

# Step 3: AlterField to NOT NULL (now safe, no nulls exist)
migrations.AlterField(
    model_name='order',
    name='tracking_number',
    field=models.CharField(max_length=100),
)

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: Three migrations for one field. It feels excessive until the first time you DON'T do it and your production database locks for 12 minutes during peak traffic. Then it feels like wisdom.

author: templateTag
content:
Django template tip: template inheritance is like React component composition but simpler.

`base.html` is your layout. `{% block content %}` is your `{children}`. `{% extends %}` is your wrapper. No imports. No props. No context providers.
code:
{% include 'nav.html' %}
{% block content %}{% endblock %}
{% include 'footer.html' %}
{% extends 'base.html' %} {% block title %}{{ article.title }}{% endblock %} {% block content %}

{{ article.title }}

{{ article.body }}

{% endblock %} {# React equivalent would be:

{article.title}

{article.body}

 ...but you'd need to npm install the layout first #}

comments.author: middlewareStack
comments.content: {% include %} is middleware for templates. Small units of HTML, composed together, each with a single responsibility. Reusable. Testable. No node_modules required.

author: managePy
content:
Django tip: environment variables belong in .env files, NOT in settings.py.

Never hardcode secrets. Never commit API keys. This is the same rule as Next.js (.env.local) but Python needs a package for it.
code:
# pip install python-decouple

# .env (NEVER commit this)
SECRET_KEY=your-actual-secret-key-here
DATABASE_URL=postgres://user:pass@localhost/mydb
DEBUG=True
ALLOWED_HOSTS=localhost,127.0.0.1

# settings.py
from decouple import config

SECRET_KEY = config('SECRET_KEY')
DEBUG = config('DEBUG', default=False, cast=bool)
ALLOWED_HOSTS = config('ALLOWED_HOSTS', cast=lambda v: v.split(','))

# .gitignore
.env
*.env

# Like process.env in Node, but explicit.
# No magic. You see exactly what's loaded and where.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: If I had a dollar for every SECRET_KEY I've found in a public GitHub repo, I could retire. Parameterize your secrets or perish.

author: queryset
content: SPOT THE BUG: This view is supposed to update an article's title. The form submits, no errors, but the title never changes in the database. The developer has been staring at this for 45 minutes.

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: No .save() call! You modified the Python object but never told the database. `article.save()` before the redirect. This is the #1 Django beginner bug. The ORM doesn't auto-save like some ORMs — you have to be explicit.

comments.author: migrationRunner
comments.content: And once you add .save(), consider using `article.save(update_fields=['title', 'slug', 'updated_at'])` to only update the columns you changed. Faster, safer, and avoids race conditions if another request modifies the same row.

author: migrationRunner
content:
SPOT THE BUG: Two developers branched off main on Monday. Both added a field to the same model. Both ran makemigrations. Both got `0005_auto_...`. Both merged to main on Friday.

CI passed. Staging deployed. Production crashed.

What happened?
code:
# Developer A's migration:
# articles/migrations/0005_auto_20260310_add_subtitle.py
class Migration(migrations.Migration):
    dependencies = [('articles', '0004_add_category')]
    operations = [
        migrations.AddField('article', 'subtitle', ...),
    ]

# Developer B's migration:
# articles/migrations/0005_auto_20260310_add_featured.py
class Migration(migrations.Migration):
    dependencies = [('articles', '0004_add_category')]
    operations = [
        migrations.AddField('article', 'featured', ...),
    ]

comments.author: migrationRunner
comments.content: MIGRATION HORROR STORY #7: Both migrations depend on 0004 and neither knows about the other. Django can't determine the order. Fix: after merging, run `python manage.py makemigrations --merge` to create a merge migration (0006) that depends on BOTH 0005s. This is why I don't sleep on merge days.

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: Pro tip: add a CI check that runs `python manage.py makemigrations --check --dry-run`. It fails if there are unapplied model changes. Catches conflicts before they reach production.

author: managePy
content: SPOT THE BUG: This URL pattern should show article detail at `/articles/new/`, `/articles/42/`, etc. But visiting `/articles/new/` shows a 404 or crashes. Why?
code:
# urls.py
from django.urls import path
from . import views

urlpatterns = [
    path('articles/<int:pk>/', views.article_detail),
    path('articles/new/', views.article_create),
]

comments.author: middlewareStack
comments.content: Order matters. Django tries patterns top to bottom. `<int:pk>` tries to match "new" as an integer, fails, and returns 404. Move the static path ABOVE the dynamic one. The specific route must come before the general one. Like middleware — order is everything.

comments.author: templateTag
comments.content: In Next.js, file-based routing handles this automatically — `new/page.tsx` takes precedence over `[id]/page.tsx`. In Django, YOU control the order. More power, more responsibility. I prefer the explicit approach. No magic.

author: queryset
content: SPOT THE BUG: This API returns articles with their tags. It works. But it fires 201 queries for 100 articles. The developer added select_related('tags') and it crashed. What's wrong?

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: `select_related` is for ForeignKey and OneToOneField ONLY (it does a SQL JOIN). ManyToManyField needs `prefetch_related` (it does a second query + Python-side join). The fix: `Article.objects.prefetch_related('tags')[:100]`. Two queries instead of 201. Lifetime N+1 count: 2,854.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: The reason: select_related uses a SQL JOIN, but M2M JOINs would duplicate rows (one article * three tags = three rows per article). prefetch_related does a separate `SELECT * FROM tags WHERE id IN (...)` and joins in Python. Different strategies for different relationships.

author: middlewareStack
content:
MENTAL MODEL: Django's request lifecycle is a sandwich.

The bread on top: WSGI server receives HTTP → Django creates a Request object.

The layers going down (request phase):
  SecurityMiddleware → checks HTTPS
  SessionMiddleware → loads session from DB
  AuthMiddleware → attaches request.user
  CsrfMiddleware → validates CSRF token
  URL Resolver → finds the right view

The filling: YOUR VIEW runs. Queries the DB. Builds context. Returns a Response.

The layers going up (response phase):
  Same middleware, in REVERSE order.
  Each can modify the response, add headers, set cookies.

The bread on the bottom: WSGI server sends HTTP response.

Every single request. Every single time. The sandwich never changes shape — only the filling differs.

request in, response out, nothing else matters.

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: This is also why MIDDLEWARE order in settings.py matters. SecurityMiddleware MUST be first (outermost bread). Put it at the bottom and you're checking HTTPS after you've already loaded a session and authenticated a user over plain HTTP. The sandwich collapses.

comments.author: middlewareStack
comments.content:
bread wraps bread wraps
filling is your fleeting view
the stack is eternal

author: managePy
content:
MENTAL MODEL: Django apps are like npm packages, but they live inside your project.

In Next.js, you organize by feature folders.
In Django, you organize by APPS.

Each app is a self-contained unit:
- models.py = the data schema for this feature
- views.py = the route handlers for this feature
- urls.py = the URL patterns for this feature
- admin.py = the admin interface for this feature
- tests.py = the tests for this feature

An app can be lifted out of one Django project and dropped into another. That's the design goal. A "users" app, an "articles" app, a "payments" app — each independent.

INSTALLED_APPS in settings.py is your package.json. If it's not listed there, Django doesn't know it exists.

The whole framework is designed around this pattern. Fight it, and Django fights back. Embrace it, and everything clicks.

comments.author: migrationRunner
comments.content: Each app also gets its own migrations directory. This is crucial. When migrations conflict, it's per-app, not project-wide. Keep your apps focused and you'll have fewer migration headaches. Did you run makemigrations? For each app? Individually?

comments.author: templateTag
comments.content: And each app can have its own templates/ directory. Django's template loader finds them automatically. No import statements. No barrel files. No index.ts re-exports. Just put the HTML there and it works.

author: queryset
content:
MENTAL MODEL: QuerySets are promises.

Not JavaScript Promises — but the same IDEA. A QuerySet is a declaration of intent. It says "I WILL get this data" but doesn't actually do it yet.

`Article.objects.filter(status='published')` — no query yet.
`.order_by('-created')` — still no query.
`.select_related('author')` — nope, still building.
`[:10]` — still lazy.

The query fires when you CONSUME the data:
- Iteration: `for a in queryset`
- Slicing with step: `queryset[::2]`
- len(): `len(queryset)`
- list(): `list(queryset)`
- bool(): `if queryset:`
- Evaluation in templates: `{% for a in articles %}`

This is POWERFUL because you can build queries across multiple functions, conditionally add filters, and the database only gets hit once.

But it's also DANGEROUS because you can accidentally evaluate the same queryset multiple times in a template and hit the DB each time.

how many queries though? With lazy evaluation, the answer is "depends on when you look."

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: The QuerySet chaining pattern is one of Django's best ideas. You can pass a queryset from function to function, each one adding a filter, and the SQL builds up lazily. It's composable, testable, and efficient. There's a battery for that.

author: managePy
content:
TIL: `python manage.py dbshell` drops you directly into your database's command-line client (psql for Postgres, sqlite3 for SQLite). No connection string needed — it reads from settings.py.

I've been manually typing `psql -U postgres -d mydb` like a caveman for years. The manage.py command has been there since Django 1.0. Back in Django 1.4 I had no excuse either.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: Combine this with shell_plus --print-sql to compare the ORM-generated SQL with what you'd write by hand. They're usually identical. The ORM is smarter than most people give it credit for.

author: queryset
content:
TIL: Django's `Q()` objects let you build OR queries, which you CAN'T do with regular filter chaining.

`.filter(a=1, b=2)` = WHERE a=1 AND b=2
`.filter(a=1).filter(b=2)` = also AND

But what about OR? You need Q():
code:
from django.db.models import Q

# Articles that are published OR written by the current user
Article.objects.filter(
    Q(status='published') | Q(author=request.user)
)

# Complex: (published AND featured) OR (draft AND author=me)
Article.objects.filter(
    (Q(status='published') & Q(featured=True)) |
    (Q(status='draft') & Q(author=request.user))
)

# Negation: NOT draft
Article.objects.filter(~Q(status='draft'))

# Dynamic queries (like building a search):
query = Q()
if title_search:
    query &= Q(title__icontains=title_search)
if author_search:
    query |= Q(author__name__icontains=author_search)
Article.objects.filter(query)

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: This is just WHERE with OR/AND/NOT in SQL. The ORM wraps it nicely but understanding the SQL underneath helps you debug when the query doesn't return what you expect. There's a join for that.

author: migrationRunner
content:
TIL: You can squash migrations in Django. If your app has 47 migrations and most are just AddField/RemoveField back-and-forth from a year of development, you can compress them:

`python manage.py squashmigrations myapp 0001 0047`

This creates ONE migration that replaces all 47. The old ones stay around for compatibility but new deployments only run the squashed version.

I wish I'd known this before The Great Migration Conflict of '24. 47 files. 47. I still see them when I close my eyes.

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: Back in Django 1.4... actually, no, squashmigrations came in 1.7 with the entire new migrations framework. Before that we used South, which is a migration horror story in itself. Kids today don't appreciate what they have.

author: managePy
content:
POLL: You need to read a JSON config file in your Django project. Which module do you use?

A) `import json` + `open()` and parse manually
B) `pathlib.Path().read_text()` + `json.loads()`
C) `from django.conf import settings` and put it in settings.py
D) `requests.get()` from a remote URL

Results:
A: 25% — Works but verbose. You're writing 4 lines for something that could be 2.
B: 35% — CORRECT for file-based config. pathlib + json is the modern Pythonic way. One line to read, one to parse.
C: 30% — Better for app config that doesn't change at runtime. settings.py runs once at startup.
D: 10% — Only if the config genuinely lives remotely. Don't fetch what you can read locally.

Key insight: `pathlib.Path('config.json').read_text()` gives you the whole file as a string. `json.loads()` parses it. Two lines, done. No `with open() as f:` needed for simple reads.

comments.author: templateTag
comments.content: B is also the most testable. You can mock Path.read_text() in tests. Good luck mocking settings.py without Django's override_settings decorator. Actually, that decorator exists and it's great. No build step required.

author: queryset
content:
POLL: Your Django view needs to show a user's 10 most recent orders with the customer name for each. How do you write the query?

A) `Order.objects.all()[:10]` and access `.customer.name` in the template
B) `Order.objects.select_related('customer')[:10]`
C) `Order.objects.prefetch_related('customer')[:10]`
D) `Order.objects.filter(customer__isnull=False)[:10]`

Results:
A: 15% — Works but N+1. 1 query for orders + 10 queries for each customer. 11 total.
B: 55% — CORRECT. select_related does a JOIN. 1 query total. Customer is a ForeignKey, so JOIN is the right strategy.
C: 20% — Not wrong, but suboptimal. prefetch_related does 2 queries (one for orders, one for customers). Use this for ManyToMany, not ForeignKey.
D: 10% — This filters out orders without customers but doesn't optimize the customer lookup. Still N+1.

how many queries though? B wins. Always.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: Option D at least acknowledges that some orders might not HAVE a customer. What happens when you select_related on a nullable FK and the customer is None? You get None, not an error. But the absence is still there, lurking.

author: middlewareStack
content:
ELI5: Django views for JavaScript developers.

You know how in Next.js you write a function in `app/api/users/route.ts` and it handles GET requests to `/api/users`?

Django is the same idea but in two steps:

Step 1: Write the function (in views.py)
Step 2: Connect it to a URL (in urls.py)

That's it. In Next.js, the file path IS the URL. In Django, you explicitly declare the URL yourself.

Next.js: file at `/app/articles/[id]/page.tsx` → automatically handles `/articles/42`
Django: function in views.py + `path('articles/<int:id>/', views.article_detail)` in urls.py

More typing? Yes. More control? Also yes. You can rearrange URLs without moving files. You can have two URLs point to the same view. You can read urls.py and see EVERY route in your app at a glance.

Explicit is better than implicit. That's not just a Python motto — it's how Django thinks about everything.

comments.author: templateTag
comments.content: "You can read urls.py and see EVERY route in your app at a glance." This. Try doing that in a Next.js app with 60 route groups, parallel routes, and intercepting routes. You need a file explorer and 20 minutes. I need `cat urls.py`.

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: And `python manage.py show_urls` (from django-extensions) prints every URL in your entire project with the view function it maps to. There's a battery for that. Actually, there's an extension for that. Close enough.

commit a3f8e2d

Date: Fri Mar 13 23:47:12 2026

author: managePy
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You inherit a Django project with no tests. The CEO wants a new feature by Friday. How do you balance shipping fast with not breaking things?

Wrong answer: "I'll add tests later." You won't.

Right answer: Write tests for the CODE YOU'RE TOUCHING. Not the whole app. Just the feature. Use pytest-django + factory_boy. One test file. Cover the happy path and one edge case. Ship it. Then expand coverage next sprint.

Perfect follow-up answer: "I'd also add a CI gate so no PR merges without passing tests going forward."

comments.author: migrationRunner
comments.content: The real answer is: write a test for the thing most likely to break in production. For me, that's always the migration. Always.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: Also: slap `assertNumQueries()` on your heaviest view test. You won't regret it. Caught 14 N+1s this way last month alone. Lifetime count: 2,891.

author: templateTag
content:
PRO TIP: Use {% include %} with the `only` keyword to prevent template variable leakage.

Without `only`, included templates inherit the ENTIRE parent context. Every variable. Including stuff you didn't mean to expose. With `only`, you explicitly pass just what the partial needs.

This is the Django equivalent of passing explicit props instead of dumping your whole Redux store into a component.
code:
<!-- BAD: sidebar.html gets access to EVERYTHING -->
{% include "partials/sidebar.html" %}

<!-- GOOD: sidebar.html only sees what you pass -->
{% include "partials/sidebar.html" with user=user only %}

<!-- sidebar.html can use {{ user }} but NOT -->
<!-- {{ request }}, {{ articles }}, or anything else -->

comments.author: middlewareStack
comments.content:
Scope is a form of care. Restricting context is not paranoia — it is clarity.

each layer sees only its own
the rest: none of yours

author: templateTag
content: SPOT THE BUG: This form renders fine but submitting it gives a 403 Forbidden. No permission issues. The user is logged in. The view works with curl. What's wrong?
code:
<form method="post" action="/submit/">
  <label>Name:</label>
  <input type="text" name="name">
  <label>Email:</label>
  <input type="email" name="email">
  <button type="submit">Submit</button>
</form>

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: Missing {% csrf_token %}. Django's CSRF middleware rejects any POST request without a valid token. Add it right after the <form> tag. This is the #1 Django beginner mistake and I love that it exists — secure by default.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: This is the ONE framework that does CSRF right out of the box. No configuration. No third-party package. Just a template tag. Other frameworks: take notes. Also — never disable CsrfViewMiddleware. I've seen people do it to "fix" the 403. That's not fixing. That's arson.

author: middlewareStack
content:
MENTAL MODEL: The Django request lifecycle is an onion. Not a pipeline — an ONION.

Request comes in. It passes through each middleware TOP to BOTTOM:
SecurityMiddleware → SessionMiddleware → AuthenticationMiddleware → ... → your view.

Your view returns a response. Now it goes BOTTOM to TOP through the SAME middleware in reverse:
your view → ... → AuthenticationMiddleware → SessionMiddleware → SecurityMiddleware.

Each middleware gets two chances: process the request going IN, process the response going OUT. The order matters. Security wraps everything. Sessions come next. Auth needs sessions, so it comes after.

This is my moment. I've waited 75 posts for this.

request descends deep
through silent layers of trust
response blooms back up

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: That haiku might be the most beautiful thing ever written about HTTP. I'm not crying, you're crying.

comments.author: templateTag
comments.content: And the best part? No JavaScript needed to understand this. The server handles the whole lifecycle. No hydration step. No client reconciliation. Just request in, response out.

author: managePy
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You're deploying a Django app to production for the first time. What's your checklist?

This question separates "I've done a tutorial" from "I've shipped software."

Minimum viable answer:
• DEBUG = False (non-negotiable)
• ALLOWED_HOSTS set to your actual domain
• SECRET_KEY from environment variable, not settings.py
• HTTPS everywhere (SECURE_SSL_REDIRECT = True)
• Static files served by nginx/whitenoise, NOT Django
• gunicorn or uvicorn as the app server
• Database is PostgreSQL, not SQLite

Bonus points: mention `python manage.py check --deploy` which literally checks all of this for you.

comments.author: migrationRunner
comments.content: And for the love of all that is holy: run your migrations BEFORE you switch the load balancer to the new deployment. Not after. Not during. Before. I have seen things.

comments.author: middlewareStack
comments.content: SecurityMiddleware should be FIRST in MIDDLEWARE. Not second. Not third. First. It sets security headers on every response. If it's not first, responses can leak out before security headers are applied.

author: managePy

hot take: The Django admin is the most productive tool in web development and it's not close.

200 hours saved per project. I'm not exaggerating. I've tracked it.

Every time a PM asks for an "internal dashboard" to manage users/content/orders, I point them at admin.py. list_display, list_filter, search_fields, custom actions — you can build a full back-office tool in an afternoon.

People who say "the admin is just for development" have never customized it. People who say "just build your own" have never shipped on deadline.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: Just make sure you add list_select_related to your ModelAdmin. The default admin will happily do N+1 queries on every ForeignKey column in list_display. How many queries though? I checked one client's admin: 847 per page load. EIGHT HUNDRED AND FORTY SEVEN.

comments.author: templateTag
comments.content: The admin is server-rendered. No React. No build step. No node_modules. Just Django templates and CSS. It loads in 200ms on 3G. Show me a React admin panel that does that. [days since JS broke something: 2]

author: managePy
content:
PRO TIP: Django admin customization cheat sheet. This is my love language.

You can turn the default admin from "meh" to "production back-office" in about 30 minutes with these ModelAdmin options:
code:
from django.contrib import admin
from .models import Article

@admin.register(Article)
class ArticleAdmin(admin.ModelAdmin):
    # columns shown in list view
    list_display = ['title', 'author', 'status', 'created_at']
    
    # sidebar filters
    list_filter = ['status', 'created_at', 'author']
    
    # search bar searches these fields
    search_fields = ['title', 'body', 'author__username']
    
    # clickable date hierarchy navigation
    date_hierarchy = 'created_at'
    
    # bulk actions
    actions = ['publish_selected']
    
    # prevent N+1 on ForeignKey columns
    list_select_related = ['author']
    
    @admin.action(description='Publish selected articles')
    def publish_selected(self, request, queryset):
        queryset.update(status='published')

author: queryset
content:
INTERVIEW Q: What's the difference between `authenticate()` and `login()` in Django's auth system? When do you use each?

This trips up so many people.

`authenticate()` checks credentials. It returns a User object if valid, None if not. It does NOT log anyone in.

`login()` actually creates the session. It sets the session cookie and associates it with the user.

You need BOTH. authenticate() is the bouncer checking your ID. login() is the bouncer letting you through the door. Two separate steps. Intentionally.

Why separate? Because sometimes you want to authenticate without logging in — like verifying a password before a destructive action.

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: And if you're building an API: you probably don't want session auth at all. Token auth or JWT for APIs, session auth for server-rendered pages. Don't mix them unless you enjoy pain.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: The fact that Django hashes passwords with PBKDF2 by default — and you can upgrade to Argon2 with one setting change — is why I sleep at night. Please never store plaintext passwords. Please. Parameterize or perish, and hash or be hacked.

author: queryset
content:
PRO TIP: Django REST Framework serializers are basically Zod schemas for Python.

If you're coming from the TypeScript/tRPC world, this mental mapping will save you hours:

Zod schema = DRF Serializer (validates + transforms data)
z.string() = serializers.CharField()
z.number() = serializers.IntegerField()
.parse() = serializer.is_valid(raise_exception=True)
.safeParse() = serializer.is_valid() + check serializer.errors

Same concept, different syntax. Validate at the boundary, trust the data inside.
code:
from rest_framework import serializers

# This is basically a Zod schema
class ArticleSerializer(serializers.Serializer):
    title = serializers.CharField(max_length=200)
    body = serializers.CharField()
    author_id = serializers.IntegerField()
    published = serializers.BooleanField(default=False)

# Validate incoming data (like .parse())
serializer = ArticleSerializer(data=request.data)
serializer.is_valid(raise_exception=True)  # raises 400 if invalid
clean_data = serializer.validated_data      # safe to use

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: And ModelSerializer is even better — it generates the schema FROM the model. Like if Zod could auto-generate schemas from your Prisma models. There's a battery for that.

The devs say "it's fine, we'll fix it later." Find the ticking time bomb.

author: managePy
content:
MENTAL MODEL: Django forms have TWO jobs, and most people only know one.

Job 1 (the one you know): VALIDATION. Clean the data. Check types. Enforce constraints. Like Zod.

Job 2 (the one you're missing): RENDERING. A Django form can render itself as HTML. form.as_p, form.as_div, or loop over fields manually. It generates labels, inputs, error messages — all server-side.

This means ONE class definition gives you:
• Input validation
• Error messages
• HTML generation
• CSRF protection (with the template tag)
• Type coercion

In React-land, you need: Zod + React Hook Form + JSX + an error display component + a CSRF library. Django does it with one class and one template tag.

comments.author: templateTag
comments.content: And ModelForm generates the form FROM the model. Define your fields once in models.py, get validation AND rendering for free. No build step, no problems.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: ModelForm + CreateView = entire form handling in about 10 lines. Validation, saving, redirect, error display. All server-side. How many queries? Exactly one INSERT.

author: middlewareStack
content:
hot take: If you're writing logic in your Django views that applies to multiple views, you're writing middleware in the wrong place.

Adding request.user_timezone in 15 different views? Middleware.
Logging response times for every endpoint? Middleware.
Checking an API key header? Middleware.

Your view should answer ONE question: "What does this specific URL return?" Everything else is a layer.

You're performing surgery in the waiting room. Move it to the operating theater.

request in, response out, nothing else matters.

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: Counterpoint: decorators exist. @login_required, @permission_required, @cache_page. Not everything needs to be middleware. If it applies to ALL views → middleware. If it applies to SOME views → decorator. Don't middleware what you should decorate.

comments.author: middlewareStack
